summary: Gruber examines the rumored naming scheme for Apple's 2018 iPhone lineup — iPhone XS, XS Max, and XR — and finds most of the names awkward, particularly when the X is supposed to be pronounced as 'ten.' He notes that 'Max' rather than 'Plus' makes some sense since the larger OLED model reportedly differs only in size, not camera capabilities. He floats his own preferred naming scheme of 'iPhone' and 'iPhone Pro' mirroring iPad and MacBook conventions, but acknowledges why that wouldn't work well for an annual product cycle. He concludes that while 'XR' seems unlikely given its lack of sourcing and odd positioning for a budget model, the name he's heard is supposedly 'surprising.'
key_points: The 'Plus' branding made sense historically because larger iPhones had better cameras and displays, not just bigger screens — 'Max' fits better when size is the only difference
Gruber's preferred naming scheme was 'iPhone' and 'iPhone Pro' to match Apple's iPad and MacBook conventions, but iPhones' strict annual cadence makes year-agnostic naming awkward
The 'XR' name lacks credible sourcing compared to 'XS Max' and seems poorly positioned for a model costing $200 less than the XS
The obvious choice for the LCD model would be 'iPhone 9' but Gruber has heard the actual name is 'surprising'
None of the rumored names work well if X is pronounced 'ten' — a complaint Gruber has been consistent about since the iPhone X launched
money_quotes: None of these names make sense to me if we're supposed to pronounce the X as 'ten', but I said the same thing last year.
The previous 'Plus' iPhone always had more to offer than sheer display size. They were 'plus' in more ways than size.
This seems nutty to me, because it doesn't sound like a product that should cost $200 less than the 'XS'.
My original guess, before any of these rumors began dropping last week, was that Apple would call the 6.1-inch LCD model 'iPhone' and both of the OLED models 'iPhone Pro'.
If Apple ever does use the name 'iPhone Pro', it would make way more sense to start using that name with a significant form factor change.
There is something about iPads and MacBooks that makes it seem OK to just keep using the exact same names that wouldn't seem right with iPhones.
key_insight: Apple's iPhone naming has painted itself into a corner where the X-as-ten pronunciation creates awkward product names, and the annual release cadence prevents adopting the simpler naming conventions used for iPads and MacBooks.
